trace.extraction_context_preview: ## Introduction Oxygen has been used in neonatal intensive care units (NICUs) since the 1940s, and its application was later associated with retinopathy of prematurity (ROP) 1–3. Pulse oximeters that monitor oxygen saturation (SpO2) have been routinely used in most NICUs since the 1980s to guide manual adjustments of the fraction of inspired oxygen (FiO2). However, manually controlling FiO2 is often time-consuming and untimely in preterm infants, which increases the risks of associated side effects affecting the eyes, lungs and central nervous system 4–6. Retinopathy of prematurity is the most important cause of blindness in the city of Cali, Colombia, 7 and accounts for 34% of all blind children in the city. --- ## Introduction Despite recently published randomised trials 8–11, we still do not know what the ideal SpO2 target is for all infants. Outcome assessment was editor-assigned quality ratings recorded objectively in the editorial management system; editors selected reviewers in usual fashion and were reportedly unaware of which new reviewers were assigned to mentorship or control groups, so measurement is unlikely to be influenced by knowledge of allocation.

domain_rows.support_text: Quote: "Four subjects randomized to the mentorship group never made contact of any kind with their mentors, and thus did not receive the intended intervention. We therefore present below the per-protocol analysis excluding these four subjects; however we also performed a corresponding intention-to-treat analysis that yielded essentially identical results (data not shown)." Comment: The authors acknowledge 4 randomized participants did not receive the intervention but report an ITT analysis that produced essentially identical results to per-protocol. Missing data appear small and balanced such that bias is unlikely. Probably done: ITT performed and missing outcomes unlikely to materially affect results.
